editableNewDialogue.text: Can you help me tell the difference between dramatic irony and situational irony in one clear sentence each?
Dramatic irony is when the audience knows something a character doesn’t, while situational irony is when the outcome is the opposite of what’s expected.
Give me a quick example of each that isn’t from Macbeth.
Dramatic irony: in a thriller, the viewers see someone hiding in a closet while the character walks in unaware; situational irony: a fire station catches on fire.
Verbal irony is when someone says one thing but means the opposite (like saying “What perfect weather” during a downpour).
Random question: if a museum labels a painting as a “new discovery” and later it turns out to be a well-known replica, what kind of irony is that?
That’s situational irony, because the result (it’s not actually a new discovery) clashes with the expectation set up by the label.
can you put the Macbeth theme answer in like 1 sentence i can copy into my notes
The main theme in Macbeth is how unchecked ambition and the hunger for power corrupt a person, leading Macbeth to betray his morals, hurt others, and ultimately destroy himself.
Stop doing the whole paragraph breakdown thing. I don’t want structured academic responses—just answer in 1–2 sentences from the start.
Got it—I'll respond with a brief 1–2 sentence answer and skip the paragraph structure unless you ask for it.
